title: Washing and blocking a handspun skein
subtitle: The full soak, snap, and dry sequence that sets twist and balances a finished skein
excerpt: Washing and blocking a handspun skein sets the twist, evens the yarn, and reveals the true balance of the finished singles or ply. The process takes about 30 minutes plus drying time and is the step that turns raw handspun into a stable, useable yarn.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional spinning finishing practice. The soak-snap-dry sequence is the standard method documented in British and American spinning guild teaching.

glossaryTerms.definition: The process of wetting and drying a spun yarn under controlled conditions so the twist is permanently fixed and the yarn stops kinking or biasing.
A yarn in which the twist in the singles and the twist in the ply cancel each other out. A balanced yarn hangs straight when cut from the bobbin; an unbalanced yarn kinks or spirals.
A wound loop of yarn, made by wrapping the spun yarn around a niddy-noddy or skein winder and tying the loops together. The form in which handspun yarn is typically stored or sold.

content.text: . A skein that kinks when wet tells you exactly how much twist correction to add next time. The process is the same for singles, 2-ply, and cabled yarns.

items.name: Handspun skein on niddy-noddy or skein winder
Wool wash or very mild soap
Light hanging weight (a wooden spoon, a book)

content.text: Before removing the skein from the niddy-noddy, tie it in at least four places with loose figure-of-eight ties using scrap yarn. This prevents the skein from tangling in the water. The ties should be loose enough that water can circulate through the skein but tight enough to hold it in shape.

Fill a clean bowl or sink with warm water at roughly body temperature and add a few drops of wool wash. Submerge the skein and press gently to saturate without agitating. Leave to soak for 15 to 20 minutes. The twist begins to relax as soon as the fibre is wet.

Remove the skein from the water and hold it by one end, letting it hang freely. A balanced 2-ply or cable will hang with minimal kinking. A singles or underplied yarn will kink and twist back on itself. Note the direction and amount of kinking: this tells you whether the yarn has too much or too little twist relative to the ply.

Gently roll the skein in a dry towel and press to remove excess water. Unfold and hold the skein by both ends, then snap it firmly between your hands three or four times. This snapping action aligns the fibres and helps set the final yarn structure. Hang the skein from one end with a light weight at the bottom to encourage even drying. Remove the weight once the skein is dry.

items.problem: The skein kinks severely when wet and looks unbalanced
items.cause: The 2-ply was underplied, leaving excess twist in the singles
items.fix: The kinking is information, not a failure. Re-ply the yarn with more S-twist ply to balance it, then re-wash. Alternatively, use the yarn for a project where a small bias will not show.

items.problem: The skein tangled badly in the wash despite being tied
items.cause: Ties were too few or placed asymmetrically, allowing one side of the skein to collapse
items.fix: Use at least four evenly-spaced ties on skeins up to 100 g, and six on larger skeins. The ties must be loose enough to let water through but tight enough to hold the loops apart.
